blocks.body: Present Perfect: Common Mistakes helps you avoid mixing a present result with finished time and choose been / gone, for / since, and yet / already / just more accurately.

{"level":"A2-B1","intro":"Mistakes with the Present Perfect usually come from choosing the wrong form for experience or a result now versus a finished moment in the past.","rules":[{"label":"RESULT NOW","color":"emerald","text":"Use the Present Perfect when a past action still connects to the present moment.","example":"I have lost my keys. (= I do not have them now.)"},{"label":"FINISHED TIME","color":"blue","text":"When the sentence has yesterday, last week, in 2024, two days ago, you usually need the Past Simple.","example":"I saw him yesterday."},{"label":"DETAILS THAT CHANGE THE FORM","color":"amber","text":"Check been / gone, for / since, and yet / already / just carefully because many repeated mistakes live there.","example":"She has been to Rome. / She has gone to Rome."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. Where Learners Most Often Get Lost
blocks.body:
The most common problem is using the Present Perfect just because the action happened in the past. What matters is the connection to now.

  • Present Perfect: experience, result now, unfinished period, news - I have finished the report.
  • Past Simple: finished moment or clear past time - I finished the report yesterday.
  • Been means a person visited a place and came back; gone means the person is still away.
  • For shows duration, while since shows the starting point.

blocks.heading: 2. How the Correct Choice Works
blocks.body:
When you are unsure, follow a short checking route.

  1. Is there a finished time marker? If yes, you probably need the Past Simple.
  2. Does the result matter now? If yes, check the Present Perfect.
  3. Is the person still there? Then gone often fits. If they came back, use been.
  4. Are you talking about duration or a starting point? -> for three years, but since 2022.
  5. Is it a negative or question about completion? -> yet is common; in positive sentences already often works for early completion, and just for a very recent action.

{"title":"3. Common mistakes","items":[{"label":"Mistake 1","color":"rose","title":"Using the Present Perfect with a finished time marker.","wrong":"I have seen him yesterday.","right":"I saw him yesterday.","hint":"Words such as yesterday, last week, in 2023, two hours ago usually move the sentence to the Past Simple."},{"label":"Mistake 2","color":"amber","title":"Mixing up been and gone for experience.","wrong":"My sister has gone to Paris three times.","right":"My sister has been to Paris three times.","hint":"For life experience or a visit with a return, use been. Gone means the person has not come back yet."},{"label":"Mistake 3","color":"sky","title":"Confusing for and since.","wrong":"We have lived here since three years.","right":"We have lived here for three years.","hint":"For + duration, since + starting point: since 2022, since Monday."},{"label":"Mistake 4","color":"rose","title":"Using already instead of yet in a typical negative pattern.","wrong":"I have not finished already.","right":"I have not finished yet.","hint":"In negatives and questions about completion, yet is usually the better choice. Already appears more often in positive sentences."}]}

{"title":"4. Quick takeaways","items":["If the sentence includes finished time, you usually need the Past Simple.","If the important point is the result now, check the Present Perfect.","Use been for experience with a return and gone for someone who is still away.","Remember the core pairs: for + duration, since + starting point, yet in negatives and questions, already / just with their own meanings."]}
